Lazy spam musubi (warning, unhealthy, high sodium)
1 cup rice
1 egg
1/3 can Spam, cubed
2 sheets of dried seaweed

Cook rice, mix in egg as soon as it's finished so it cooks with the residual heat, cook spam crispy then mix it in, cut seaweed into little strips then mix those in, then close the lid on the rice cooker to let the seaweed soften up. Spice to your liking, I usually just use salt and a bit of soy sauce. For authenticity, eat straight out of the rice cooker bowl.

Two Oriental struggle meals, both using marked-down, day-old sushi.  (Include the pickled ginger and wasabi paste for both 'recipes'.) :

2. Sushi casserole - Dump sushi in a bowl with some water--to soften and rehydrate the rice--and microwave until hot.

Both 'recipes' taste FAR better than they have any right to.

I grew up poor, so struggle meals like franks and beans were literally just "meals". That was the food you got, because we had to make shit work. lol You learned to get creative with stuff and just combine any ingredients you could get your hands on, especially if you could get it half price. I wanted to have a baked potato for lunch on the weekend, but the avocados I grabbed on sale had finally ripened. So I just mashed them together with some margarine, salt, and pepper, and then stuffed that back into the potato skin. Melted a little cheese on top as well for to be safe. Needed a bit more tuning, but hella healthy and the avocado adds a pleasant, almost grassy flavour to the potato.

I have a pretty simple "pantry soup" recipe I think you'll like.

- 1 can of chili (the 'no beans' variety)
- 1 can of kidney beans (or your beans of choice)
- 1 can of corn or mixed veggies

- 1 smoked sausage (I prefer Kielbasa) chopped into halved coins
- 1-2 tbsp of olive oil
- onion and garlic powder to taste
- Optional: About 1 tbsp of hot sauce (I prefer Tapatío)
- Optional: 2-3 handfuls of oyster crackers if you like it thick

Prepare your ingredients along with a large tupperware container.
Throw everything in, mix, and toss it in the fridge until you're ready to eat.
Just heat up and serve. Makes roughly 2-3 servings depending on your appetite.

My struggle meal: Curd rice:  
ingredients
1 medium size bowl of cooked rice.
1 cup yogurt,
3-4 tablespoons of chickpea flour.
2-3 small green chilie not pepper.
2 cup water.
1/2 teaspoon mustard seed.
1/2 teaspoon cumin seed.
liitle turmeric like 1/3 teasoon.
6-7 curry leaves.
salt to taste, sugar to taste, and oil.

Procedure:
In a bowl, mix the yogurt, chickpea flour, water, and mix it well.  

next heat the oil in the pan, then add mustard seed. First, let it make that popping sound. Then add cumin seeds. The add cut pieces of small chili and curry leaves. Then add turmeric and the yogurt mixture that we prepared to the pan. Cook it till it boils for 3-4 min. Do not put it on high heat; put it on medium. Then add salt and sugar to taste. Put this cooked curd thing on the cooked rice and enjoy.

Meanwhile I’m here with 3 main struggle meals and one can’t even be considered struggle anymore because eggs are super expensive now.
1. Ramen, some carrots and maybe frozen spinach, dried mushrooms I forgot I had from when I could afford the farmer’s market, American cheese slice if I’m feeling something creamier, fried egg when they were cheap.
2. Eggs à la Goldenrod: White sauce with egg whites in it, toasted white bread, put sauce on top, then crumble egg yolks, sprinkle with paprika. Now it’s an Easter luxury and I was sick so I couldn’t even go home to family to eat it this year 🥲
3. Bought short grained rice in bulk, have some dried kombu from years ago, some sugar and some rice vinegar that my white grandmother had no idea how to use. Literally just sushi rice. Maybe some soy sauce and tuna and kewpie mayo if I could afford a can.
My biggest struggle meal though was literally combining fruits and veggies from all of the gardens of my family and friends, excess frozen trout my uncle caught, and sometimes sleep for dinner to get me through the month. My cat ate like a king, though. A fat, fat king.

My favorite struggle meal is lobster with caviar:

3 cooked lobster tails
Chopped chives, to taste
4 tablespoon butter, melted and slightly cooled
Pinch of salt
Brioche bread cut into 3 thick rectangular pieces
Oil for frying
Chives, for garnish
0.5 - 1 oz Plaza Caviar

DIRECTIONS
1. Chop lobster tails and mix in a bowl with chopped chives, butter and salt.
2. Heat oil in a skillet and pan fry brioche so that all sides are browned and crispy (be careful not to burn).
3. Top each toast with lobster, a generous spoonful of caviar and chives.
4. Don't forget to throw the rest of the useless lobster in the trash, or just have your butler take care of everything.

Soy sauce gravy

-A half cup of margerine (or butter or whatever similar thing there is) in a sauce pot on medium heat
-Melt it (if you want to be fancy add garlic or onions durring this step)
-Cook for 30 ish seconds
-Slowly whisk in a half cup of flour
-Whisk in roughly 3 cups of water (or broth, it tastes allot better if you use broth, except vegetable broth, its nasty)
-2-4 packets of soy sauce that you probably got left over from ordering chinese
-Whisk for roughly 5 mins over medium heat until it starts thickening. 

Add it to fries or whatever

Corned beef FROM THE US is nasty. Other countries have quality Corned Beef.

Anyone wants recipes, I can share one in the read more:

Forest Omelete:

Ingredients:
1 or 2 eggs (Depends on portion preferences and hunger)
Butter (for pan)
Rosmary (finely chopped and, if you can, ground in a mortor and pestile.)
1/8th Corned Beef (Store the rest in the fridge for toasties)
salt to taste

Steps:

Chop and grind up your chopped Rosmary to really awaken its power.
Cube your Corned beef into tiny cubes. Do not break it, ideally you want to maintain small cube shape.
Beat your eggs in a small bowl, mixing in your Rosmary.
Add beat eggs to buttered hot pan.
Drop in your corned beef to cook along with your egg.
Try your best to flip the egg+beef omelete.
Serve with tea or coffee.

Super simple, and makes you think of eating eggs in a fantasy setting or a bright lush forest cabin next to a waterfall.

Ingredients

Corn: Steamed and removed from the cob (about 2–3 ears worth).

Onion: 1 large, roughly chopped.

Tomato: 1 large, chopped.

Cooking Oil: Enough to coat the pan.

Seasoning: Salt (and fresh pepper if you're rich like canoe here).

Instructions

Heat the oil in a large pan over medium-high heat. Add the onions and fry them until they are translucent and just beginning to soften.

Toss in the steamed corn. Continue to stir and fry the mixture until the onions start to brown slightly 

Add the chopped tomato to the pan. Keep stirring until the tomato breaks down and coats the corn and onions.

Season with salt. If you have it, add your favorite pepper.

Best served with a glass of water (you cheap sausage!)

Rivvel Nudla (but spelled correctly in German)

1 cup flour, 1 ish tsp baking powder, water. Mix until you have a dough just dry enough to kneed in your hands. Heat non-stick pan with some butter, when it's hot add half cup water. Rivvel the dough, lol. Using your hands, roll up small 'noodles'  and put them in the pan. Grandma and her sisters could rub their hands together and enough noodles for 10 people just fell from their hands like magic. I usually just do 5-8 big noodles. Put the lid on the pan and gently simmer until the water is gone. Remove the lid and scramble 2-4 eggs into the pan and cook them while rolling the noodles around in them. Salt and pepper to taste.

Sometimes I eat them with cheese instead of the eggs.

Struggle meal under ten dollars: 
Rotisserie chicken or some chicken thighs with bone in
A carrot
A couple celery stalks
A yellow onion 
Spinach if you want
Salt
Garlic powder 
Whatever savory herbs you want
- season the chicken if it isn't rotisserie with the herbs and spices
-cover with foil and roast in the oven at 350° for about a half hour, it's okay if the chicken isn't completely done, it will cook the rest of the way during the next steps.
-put it in a big pot
-chop up your veg, you can pre cook the onion in butter if you want for a little extra flavor, don't carmelize it though
-Fill up the pot with enough water to cover all the ingredients 
-Bring to a boil and then set to a low heat to barely simmer, cover with a lid with no holes and let simmer for about an hour to two hours the longer it simmers the more flavorful the stock.

You've probably made chicken soup before but this is easy and cheap and makes about a few days worth of good eating for me

I am from the UK. 
Back when I was a struggling university student. 
I survived on £20 for a month back in 2018. 
It was just baked beans(5kg cost like £3 total at the time), loose back bacon(at the time £1 a kg now £2)(5kg), white rice(5kg at 45p per kg), eggs(15 eggs for 89p), cheap frozen sausage(costed about £1.20 a kg back then still is like £2) and frozen mixed veggies(at the time it was like 60p a kg). 
My go-to meal was baked bean egg fried rice.
I would literally microwave my rice, which made it hard like stale rice.
Then I would make a stir-fry of veggies, eggs and bacon.
Then I would add baked bean which I would cook while half-smashed with a little bit of garam masala and madras curry powder.
I would then put the rice in, and depending on the day, crisp it up or just mix and serve.

or 
I would make a 10-1 ratio congee with rice.
And add the stir-fry on top.

It is something I still make once in a while, as it is tasty, but when I had it for 1 month straight, it was hell, plus it did help me lose weight.
